label: Industry & Foundation Short Awards (compute/cloud grants, research gifts, foundation short awards)
version: Industry/foundation best-practices snapshot, mid-2026 (NVIDIA Academic Grant + Applied Research Accelerator, Google Cloud research credits, AWS Cloud Credit for Research, Microsoft Azure Research, CZI Community Fund, Wellcome short awards) — verify against the live CFP/RFA, which change quarterly

sections.title: Problem / Motivation
sections.purpose: Establish the real-world problem and why it matters now, in plain language.
mustContain: The real-world problem stated in 2-4 sentences
Why it matters now (the 'so-what'), intelligible to a non-specialist reviewer
No literature review or jargon-dense opening

sections.title: Approach / What You'll Build
sections.purpose: State the methods and the concrete artifact you will produce.
mustContain: The concrete artifact (tool, model, benchmark, pipeline, dataset, or demonstrable result)
Solution-oriented description, not a literature debate
A clear end state / finite deliverable

sections.title: Why This Resource
sections.purpose: Explain what the funder's specific technology uniquely unlocks (the single most-overlooked requirement).
mustContain: Name the specific GPUs / software / models / cloud services
Map each named resource to a concrete task
Explain what it uniquely unlocks (scale, speed, otherwise-unreachable capability)
For NVIDIA: reference specific pretrained models from ai.nvidia.com and named SDKs / software distributions
Not generic 'we need compute' that could go to any vendor

sections.title: Feasibility / Preliminary Work
sections.purpose: Evidence the team can execute this scope in the award window.
mustContain: Pilot results, prototype, or directly relevant prior publications
Right-sized scope matched to demonstrated capacity and stated resources
One piece of evidence per feasibility claim

sections.title: Impact & Deliverables
sections.purpose: Specific, quantifiable outcomes plus a dissemination and sustainability path.
mustContain: Specific, quantifiable outcomes (speedup, accuracy target, dataset size, users reached, cost)
Dissemination plan (open-source repo, dataset, paper, workshop)
Adoption / sustainability path beyond the award
For applied-track awards: name the commercial/government adopter and their commitment
No grandiose or sweeping claims

sections.title: Team & Environment
sections.purpose: Briefly establish who is doing the work and why qualified.
mustContain: Who, in 2-4 sentences
Why qualified
What infrastructure / support exists

sections.title: Timeline / Milestones
sections.purpose: A few dated milestones mapped to the award window.
mustContain: A few dated, verifiable milestones inside the award period
For NVIDIA: timeline aligned to GPU-hours dates and hardware ship dates
An end state, not a vague phase list

sections.title: Budget / Resource Justification
sections.purpose: Tie the requested amount/credit to scope with a calculator-backed estimate.
mustContain: The requested amount/credit tied to the scope
For AWS/Google: a pricing-calculator link/estimate substantiating the ask
Justification of chosen machine / GPU types vs. estimated usage
No round-max 'to be safe' ask; no admin/operational/IT-ops or general lab costs

reviewCriteria: Concrete, finite deliverables with a clear end state
Feasibility over ambition — can this team finish this scope in the award window
Why THIS resource specifically — distinctive use of the funder's named technology/models/services
Alignment with the funder's mission / strategy / technology
Real-world / industry impact and dissemination (open-source release, adoption by a commercial/government partner)
A right-sized, justified ask backed by a pricing-calculator estimate

checklist.check: Proposal is within the program's page/length limit (e.g. Azure <= 3 pages; most are 1-3 pages).
Meets format rules where required: font >= 11pt, >= 0.5 inch margins, numbered pages (CZI/foundations).
All expected sections are present: problem, approach, why-this-resource, feasibility, impact/deliverables, team, timeline, budget.
Opening 2-3 sentences state problem + deliverable + impact in plain language.
'Why this resource' names specific services/GPUs/models and what each uniquely unlocks (not a generic compute ask sendable to any vendor).
For NVIDIA: references specific pretrained models / named SDKs and a clearly defined dataset.
Deliverables and outcomes are concrete and quantifiable (speedup, accuracy, dataset size, users, GPU-hours, cost).
Feasibility is evidenced by pilot data, a prototype, or relevant publications, and scope fits the award window.
Timeline has dated milestones aligned to the award / compute-hours / hardware-ship dates.
Budget/credit ask is right-sized and backed by a pricing-calculator estimate (AWS/Google) with justified machine/GPU types.
No excluded work: ongoing/established research, admin/operational/IT ops, or general lab running costs (explicit AWS exclusions).
Includes a dissemination plan (open-source, dataset, paper, workshop) and a post-award sustainability/adoption path.
Clearly aligns with the funder's mission/strategy/technology and the correct program/track.
Every scoring criterion in the CFP is explicitly addressed, in the funder's order and terms.
Every cited publication (feasibility/prior work) resolves to a real reference.
Team/environment qualifications are stated briefly (who, why qualified, what support exists).
Active voice, jargon stripped from the opening, no grandiose/sweeping claims, proofread.
Acknowledgement and reporting obligations noted (e.g. NVIDIA: acknowledge in publications, 6-month follow-up); any funder contacts named (AWS).
Current CFP/RFA read and eligibility confirmed (e.g. NVIDIA: full-time faculty at a PhD-granting institution); submitted via correct portal before deadline.

redFlags: Generic resource justification — a paragraph that could be sent to any vendor; no reason it must be this funder's tech (for NVIDIA, no demonstrated understanding of their software/models)
No nameable artifact — reviewer can't tell what exactly exists at the end
No pilot/prototype/relevant publications — feasibility doubt; scope beyond the team's capacity
Round-max ask with no pricing-calculator backing — padded budget; cost exceeds plausible benefit
Off-strategy — does not advance the funder's mission/tech, or wrong program entirely
No dissemination/adopter — nothing open-sourced, no life after the credits run out
Grandiose claims, vague timelines, over length, requested-tech never named
Requests funding for excluded categories (ongoing/established research, admin/operational/IT ops, general lab costs)
Boilerplate copied from a federal (NIH/NSF) proposal — wrong altitude, too long, too theoretical
requiredAttachments: Pricing-calculator estimate/link substantiating the ask (AWS required; Google expected)
Timeline/milestones aligned to award and (NVIDIA) compute-hours and hardware-ship dates
Submission via the program's official template/portal
recencyNote: Program tracks, award amounts, eligibility, page limits, and required forms change frequently — often quarterly (NVIDIA rotates tracks/GPU types each quarter; AWS exclusion list and Azure page cap can shift). Always read the live CFP/RFA before writing and confirm eligibility, the current scoring factors, the exact template/fields, the pricing-calculator requirement, and the deadline/portal.
